title: Digital vs. Traditional Advertising and the Recognition of Brand Intangible Assets
abstract: This paper examines how different advertising categories are associated with attributes of brand asset recognition arising in the context of acquisitions. Prior research documents that aggregate advertising is positively associated with firm sales and brand values, but it fails to consider major recent shifts in advertising from traditional channels (such as TV) to digital advertising (such as paid search and online display). Using proprietary data, I decompose advertising expenditures into the categories of traditional, online display, and paid search. Consistent with expectations, results confirm that target firms’ traditional and online display advertising exhibit a higher likelihood of brand asset recognition and higher recognized brand values as compared with paid search advertising. Confirming the economic substance of the recognized brands, additional results reveal positive investor equity market reactions to brand value, which are driven by firms investing more in traditional and online display advertising. Overall, these results confirm that certain advertising channels exhibit stronger associations with the recognition and characteristics of the underlying brand asset, consistent with their heterogeneous properties.

author.Scarlett Xiaotong Song: Peter T. Paul College of Business and Economics, University of New Hampshire, Durham, New Hampshire 03824

article.1. Introduction: This paper compares different advertising channels and assesses their relative association with characteristics of brand intangible assets in the financial statements. Recent decades have witnessed the shift of corporate investment away from tangible assets and toward intangible assets such as technology and brands. Brand assets, which are driven in large part by firms’ advertising activities, are estimated to represent over a quarter of total intangible asset value (Elsten and Hill 2017). Corresponding with the broad shift toward intangibles, advertising in recent years has witnessed an equally dramatic shift away from traditional channels (such as television) and toward digital channels (such as paid search through Google and Facebook). The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) confirms that digital advertising recently surpassed television to become the largest advertising channel, exceeding $180 billion for 2021 in the United States (IAB 2018,2021). Whereas previous studies examine R&D and innovation intangibles (e.g.,Lev and Sougiannis 1996,Wyatt 2005), less attention has been paid to advertising and brands generally, with no studies examining the implications for the shift toward digital advertising on reporting outcomes. This dearth of research likely reflects the lack of viable disclosures and data regarding firms’ advertising expenditures.1This paper uses an alternative data set that decomposes firm-level advertising expenditures, which enables the examination of how different advertising media create long-term brand value as reflected in the recognition of the intangible assets of brands on firms’ financial statements. I address this research question in the setting of mergers and acquisitions (hereafter “M&As”). Over the years, acquisitions have become an increasingly important growth strategy for companies across different industries. By acquiring other companies, businesses can quickly gain access to new markets, technologies, and customers while also expanding their product offerings and capabilities. At the same time, more and more acquisitions are being done for intangible assets than physical assets. Accounting Standards Codification (ASC) 805 requires acquirer firms to separately recognize identifiable intangible assets of an acquired target separately on the balance sheet during M&As; included in these separately recognized intangibles are brand assets. Prior literature confirms that a primary economic input that builds firms’ brand assets includes their previous advertising expenditures (e.g.,Nerlove and Arrow 1962,Barth et al. 1998).2As target firms’ brand assets must be recognized following the M&As as part of the purchase price allocation, this setting allows the direct testing of whether brand intangible assets reflect managers’ perceptions of value creation by alternative advertising channels.3 Prior marketing research suggests that different types of advertising have varying effects on firm performance (e.g.,Bayer et al. 2020). These advertising channels are divided into three broad categories: paid search, online display, and traditional.Paid searchis a type of digital advertising initiated by users: users search for a keyword on a search engine (e.g., Google or Bing) and obtain relevant ads appearing as sponsored links. This advertising possesses a high target ability (Goldfarb and Tucker 2011) and has been shown to deliver strong contemporaneous sales impact (Bayer et al. 2020), thus appearing more short-term in nature.Online displayrepresents a second type of digital advertising; it comprises online banner, plain text ads, rich media ads, and online video ads. Whereas it is sharing some characteristics of paid search—such as the ability to track and store consumer data—it is a “push” form of advertising, initiated by firms with an intent of building brand awareness (Sundar and Kim 2005).Traditional advertisingreflects all offline advertising outlets including television, magazine, newspaper, radio, and outdoor. Similar to online display, traditional advertising is initiated by firms to create positive brand associations to a wide customer audience. Thus, paid search appears most strongly associated with immediate sales impact, whereas both traditional and online display appear more reflective of longer-term value creation, which is captured by the brand asset.4 This paper exploits the above varying attributes of these advertising categories to assess their relative association with brand asset recognition. Although prior studies document the association between individual advertising expenditures and firms’ short-/long-term performance (e.g.,Hirschey and Weygandt 1985,Blake et al. 2015), they do not compare the effect of different advertising media nor consider their effects on reporting outcomes.5Using the M&A context, I predict that the recognition and value of a brand asset will have a higher association with traditional and online display expenditures relative to those for paid search. These predictions follow from the different economic roles played by these three categories of advertising expenditure. Note that inherent measurement challenges make it unclear whether managers understand and incorporate such differences across these advertising channels into their reporting of brand assets. In addition, this recognition process is further complicated by the increased uncertainty typical of M&A settings, which are the only setting in which brand assets can be recognized in financial statements. To obtain the decomposition of advertising expenditures into the three major categories, I use a proprietary data set by Kantar of annual advertising activities in multiple media channels over a 10-year period (2010–2019). Kantar is an industry-leading media-tracking company that provides firms’ advertising spending on major media outlets through systematic monitoring of firms’ advertising activities. Consistent with expectations, empirical results confirm that both traditional and online display advertising of target firms exhibit a higher association with the recognition and valuation of brand assets relative to paid search. In addition, in confirmatory evidence that acquirers’ recognition of brands reflects an underlying economic asset, I document positive investor reactions to the recognized brand assets, which appear mainly driven by target firms investing more in traditional and online display advertising. These results are robust to a battery of sensitivity tests using an expanded sample that includes private targets, a bootstrapping method, additional control variables, alternative variable measurements, joint tests using seemingly unrelated regressions, and propensity score matching and entropy balancing matching to control for target characteristics. This study makes three contributions. First, it contributes to the literature on the recognition and valuation of intangible assets. In recent years, the focus of M&As has shifted from the acquisition of physical assets, such as manufacturing plants or real estate, to the acquisition of intangible assets, such as intellectual property, technology, and brand. Prior studies focus on R&D intangibles, documenting the economic drivers and managerial incentives in intangible asset recognition (e.g.,Muller 1999,Wyatt 2005,Zhang and Zhang 2017). Few studies investigate advertising intangibles without considering the recent major shift toward digital advertising (Barth et al. 1998). This paper adds to this literature by confirming the various roles played by alternative advertising activities in recognizing brand assets and valuation. Second, this paper builds on accounting research examining the role of advertising in reporting outcomes. As the recognition and valuation of intangible assets involve significant managerial estimation, it is unclearex antewhether managers can recognize and value brands based on advertising inputs, which appear to have various roles with respect to brand characteristics. Much research uses aggregated advertising from standard data sources (such as Compustat) and thus implicitly assumes that the aggregate effects can be attributed to each of the advertising components equally (Heitzman et al. 2010,Ellis et al. 2012). A few studies consider these various channels but do so in limited financial reporting decisions (Cohen et al. 2010,Madsen and Niessner 2019). This paper builds on these studies by examining the heterogeneous effects of alternative advertising media on a key (and increasingly important) observable reporting outcome: recognized brand assets in the context of M&As. Third, there is an ongoing debate regarding the long-term effects, and thus appropriate reporting, of advertising expenditure. Extant U.S. and international standards require most advertising expenditures receive homogeneous reporting treatment: expensed as incurred. The rationale for this treatment stems primarily from uncertainty regarding the measurement of any future benefits attributable to advertising (ASC 720). By documenting the heterogeneous effects of alternative advertising channels and mapping from advertising to brand, this study offers insights regarding the translation of advertising cost into brand intangibles from the managerial perspective.6Taking together, these results provide input to standard setters relevant for reconsideration of the general reporting framework for advertising, including potential expansion of advertising categories that may qualify for capitalization. In addition, the results also suggest that managers may consider more expansive disclosure practices relative to extant practice to better inform users of the nature and heterogeneity of their advertising investments. Section 2surveys the literature and presents the hypothesis development.Section 3describes the research design.Section 4presents primary empirical results, withSection 5showing sensitivity and additional analysis.Section 6concludes.
article.2. Literature Review and Hypothesis Deve: 2.1. Prior Literature This paper builds upon three types of literature: prior research linking advertising expenditures to firm outcomes, accounting research on the reporting of mergers and acquisitions and related intangible asset recognition, and recent marketing research examining differences between digital and traditional advertising. Prior research in accounting and finance examines advertising, principally in the context of how such expenditures relate to market variables such as equity price (Grullon et al. 2004,Lou 2014,Chemmanur and Yan 2019,Madsen and Niessner 2019). Motivating much of this research is the debate regarding the appropriate accounting treatment of advertising expenditures, with current U.S. GAAP (as well as extant international reporting standards) generally requiring that such expenditures be expensed as incurred.7Earlier research supports this argument, documenting that advertising fails to generate benefits beyond one period (Bublitz and Ettredge 1989), though some research provides contrasting evidence of advertising having multiperiod benefits (Hirschey and Weygandt 1985). Whereas advertising costs are expensed under U.S. GAAP, brand assets can be recognized by acquirer firms in the context of M&As. This creates an inconsistency in the accounting treatment of internally generated versus externally acquired brand assets. The two primary requirements to recognize a separate brand asset are (i) the asset must lack physical substance and be expected to generate future economic benefits, and (ii) the asset arises either from contractual or other legal rights or is separable (Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) 2007). Brand assets are recognized apart from goodwill under either (i) contractual criteria if trademarks/internet domain names are registered with governmental agencies, or (ii) separable criteria if the trademark/trade name is capable of being separated from the entity, which FASB believes normally would be the case (Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) 2007).8Further, ASC 805 requires the intangible assets to be recognized at fair value. Prior literature documents the role of management incentives and measurement errors in intangible asset recognition during M&A (Jones 2011,Zhang and Zhang 2017,McInnis and Monsen 2023). However,Wyatt (2005)argues that whereas managerial incentives exist, the underlying economics of firms drive the first-order recognition of intangible assets. In addition,Kallapur and Kwan (2004)investigate the relevance of recognized brand assets using M&As in the United Kingdom and find that recognized brand values are positively associated with acquirers’ market value of equity. A number of studies document that total advertising increases firm sales and brand awareness (Meenaghan 1995,Barth et al. 1998). Motivated by the recent evolution of advertising expenditures toward digital advertising channels, more recent marketing research compares the impacts of different advertising channels—often disaggregating advertising into the broad categories of traditional, online display, and paid search. Traditional advertising expenditures (such as television) are perceived as primarily aimed at brand building, the effectiveness of which is measured by brand awareness and consumer preferences (De Vries et al. 2017). Online display advertising (such as banner ads) reflects some similar characteristics to traditional advertising in that online display also generates brand interest (Draganska et al. 2014), increases brand search queries (Danaher and Mullarkey 2003), helps with customer retention (Manchanda et al. 2006), and is associated with firm sales (Bayer et al. 2020). In contrast, paid search (such as Google searches) translates more directly to sales than other channels (Ghose and Yang 2009,Rutz et al. 2011). Studies comparing the effectiveness of different advertising channels document that paid search is superior to traditional in increasing short-term firm sales (Bayer et al. 2020), and online display performs as well as TV in building brand awareness (Draganska et al. 2014). There is also an emerging literature in accounting examining the advertising media channels and their implications for financial reporting decisions. For example,Cohen et al. (2010)examine the use of media advertising to meet a firm’s earnings target;Madsen and Niessner (2019)investigate the effect of print advertisement on investor attention. 2.2. Hypothesis Development Firms are faced with budget constraints that require them to allocate advertising expenditures over paid search, online display, and traditional advertising. These advertising channels differ in one key aspect—the nature of orientation, which arises from the marketing theory that advertising reaches consumers in different stages of the purchase decision-making process, or the so-called “marketing funnel.”9This funnel is typically characterized by several stages: the top of the funnel is the “awareness” stage, when firms advertise to increase product/service awareness; the middle is customer “consideration” (or interest) stage, when firms try to generate interests from consumers through advertisements; and the bottom is the “decision” (or action) stage, when the actual purchase occurs. Paid search is a type of digital advertising initiated by users who search for a keyword on a search engine (e.g., Google, Bing, or Baidu) and obtain relevant ads appearing as sponsored links (typically on the top and the right side of the search results). When users click on the sponsored ad, they are taken to the advertiser’s website. This advertising takes place deep in the funnel of the consumer purchase journey, with high personalization and a close reach to actual purchase. It also possesses a high target ability (Goldfarb and Tucker 2011) and enables firms to track and measure the effectiveness of their ads (Rutz et al. 2011). The cost of paid search advertising is determined using a cost-per-click model, where the company pays only when a user clicks on the ad. Because of its high targetability, paid search advertising could be quite costly, particularly for firms in the service industry. Consistent with these traits, paid search advertising has been shown to deliver strong contemporaneous sales impact (Bayer et al. 2020) and thus appears more short-term in nature. Online display is a second major type of digital advertising, comprising online banners, plain text ads, rich media ads, and online video ads. As another type of digital advertising, it shares some characteristics of paid search, including the ability to track and target the consumer. However, online display is a “push” form of advertising, initiated by firms with an intent of building brand awareness (Sundar and Kim 2005). The cost of online display is typically determined by the number of impressions, with one impression equivalent to a single page view by a web user. Hence, online display occurs in the earlier stages of the consumer purchase decision funnel and tries to “cast a wide net.” Traditional advertising reflects all offline advertising outlets; these include television, magazine, newspaper, radio, and outdoor (such as billboards). Similar to online display, traditional advertising is initiated by firms to create positive brand associations to a wider customer audience and usually occurs at the initial stage of the consumer purchase funnel. It typically does not allow for tracking or direct links to customer behavior. Although traditional advertising has the benefits of reaching a broader audience, its costs are relatively higher compared with digital advertising. Accordingly, the brand-building process by online display and traditional advertising is primarily aimed at influencing the awareness of a customer, who may purchase anytime within a reasonably long horizon, that is, the first stage of the funnel (Srinivasan et al. 2010). Thus, the main differences between traditional, online display, and paid search advertising lie in their targetability and trackability and their nature of orientation. Although advertising of all types could generate immediate sales as well as brand value, paid search advertising appears more sales driven (on average), whereas online display and traditional advertising reflect more brand-building investments. This suggests that firms accumulate brand value more through expenditures in traditional outlets and online display than through paid search. When firms possessing such brand value are acquired, the accumulated brand can produce ongoing long-term economic benefits for the acquirer (which, presumably, in part, motivates the acquisition). Accordingly, I predict the following: Traditional and online display, as compared with paid search, has a higher association with the likelihood of brand asset recognition by acquirers during M&As. As tension in this expectation, prior literature shows that all three advertising channels are associated with firm performance (as proxied by Tobin’s Q) (Bayer et al. 2020). If acquirer managers do not distinguish between, or are unaware of, different channels as creating differential future economic benefits for the firm, traditional and online display may not show a higher association with brand recognition than paid search. After intangible asset recognition, the acquirer firm must determine several key asset attributes: the value of the brand asset and its useful life (to determine the amount of recurring amortization, if any). To determine the asset value, the FASB guidelines recommend three approaches to valuing intangible assets: market, income, and cost approach. For brand assets, the most common approach is the income approach, specifically the relief from royalty (hereafter the “RFR”) method. The RFR method determines an asset value by reference to the hypothetical royalty payments saved through owning the asset, as compared with licensing the asset from a third party. Key inputs in the RFR method include revenue forecasts associated with the asset, expected useful life, royalty rate, and discount rate. The valuation process is typically finalized within six to 12 months of the effective date of the acquisition (ASC 805;Beneish et al. 2022). Whereas advertising expense is not an explicit input in the RFR method, it is likely that forecasting the key factors utilized in the method requires advertising input. Because traditional and online display takes place in the earlier stages of the marketing funnel, firms investing in these two advertising activities are likely to accumulate brand awareness, which may lead to sales in future periods of time. An enhanced brand awareness could also change consumers’ preferences over the products, leading to product differentiation, so that the demand function for the product becomes inelastic and firms can charge a higher price premium. Higher future product price and quantity will result in a higher future revenue stream. Furthermore, a strong brand is likely to have a higher royalty rate under licensing arrangements and can reduce product risk and uncertainty as reflected in a lower discount rate. All of these aspects can positively affect brand value under the RFR method. Moreover, as advertising expenditures represent management investment strategies and are quite sticky (Anderson et al. 2003), firms allocating money in traditional and online display are likely those that consistently invest in brand-building advertisements. Such advertisements affect consumers’ expectations of product or service quality (Kirmani and Wright 1989), leading to strong brand images and high customer loyalty (Yoo et al. 2000). Collectively, these insights lead to the second hypothesis: Traditional and online display, as compared with paid search, has a higher association with the value of brand asset recognized by acquirers during M&As. Although the intangible valuation is based on a thorough analysis by the acquirer and independent valuation experts and is subject to SEC monitoring and audit, it can still reflect measurement error and managerial discretion. For example, managers may consider aggregate advertising instead of individual components as an economic driver of brand value. In addition, managers face incentives to designate a higher brand asset value. Collectively, if measurement error and managerial discretion cause the value of the brand to deviate from its true economic value, I expect to observe no differential associations between the examined advertising components and brand value.
article.3. Research Design: 3.1. Advertising Data and Sample Collection To test the differential association between traditional, online display, and paid search advertising and recognized brand asset characteristics during M&As, I measure advertising using the Kantar data set. Kantar decomposes a firm’s advertising expenditures into 19 media outlets; thus, it is much more granular as compared with the commonly used Compustat advertising data.10Kantar obtains this information through systematic monitoring of firms’ advertising activities across various channels. It first tracks firms’ advertisement occurrences over major media outlets and then multiplies each advertisement occurrence by its related rate provided by advertising agents to get the dollar expenditure estimate.11The initial Kantar data set includes advertising expenditures for around 175,000 firms each year from 2010 to 2019; most are smaller, private firms. I match reported parent firm names from Kantar to those listed as U.S. publicly traded in Compustat, obtaining advertising expenses for 2,589 unique firms.12 Appendix Bpresents the time series trend of advertising by media outlets using all firms from Kantar. Using 2010 as a base point, digital advertising shows an increasing trend from 2010–2019, whereas newspaper and magazine advertising presents a decrease. TV and other traditional advertising remains relatively flat over the years. These statistics are consistent with the growing advertising market in digital advertising and the shift in marketing from traditional media outlets to digital outlets. Appendix Cpresents descriptive statistics using matched public firms from Kantar.Table C.1presents descriptive statistics on Kantar advertising by industry. Firms in the manufacturing, transportation, and public utilities industries, as well as wholesale and retail, spend a relatively higher amount of media advertising compared with other industries. Firms in services and public administration, manufacturing, and wholesale and retail industries invest a higher percentage of their revenue on media advertising. Comparing the relative advertising spending on traditional versus digital advertising, services and public administration industries present a higher spending on digital advertising (40% on paid search, 22% on online display, and 38% on traditional), whereas agriculture, mining, and construction industries spend more on traditional media outlets (8% on paid search, 16% on online display, and 76% on traditional). Figure C.1shows the time series evolution of digital advertising as a percentage of total Kantar in different industries by year, with two key observations regarding the advertising strategies of different industries. First, there is a rising trend of digital advertising as a proportion of total media advertising across several industries over the years. For example, the wholesale and retail industries start with 30% digital advertising in 2010, with this figure gradually increasing to 51% in 2018. Second, a few industries, such as the service and public administration industries, are spending more on digital advertising than on traditional, particularly in recent years. This suggests that these industries may be more focused on online marketing channels. These trends are consistent with firms in different industries having different advertising strategies that are shaped by their respective products, markets, audiences, and goals. Appendix Dpresents a comparison of the advertising expenses reported by Compustat versus Kantar databases in two examples. On average, the advertising expense tracked by Kantar is one-third of the amount reported in Compustat, which is consistent with the descriptive statistics inBayer et al. (2020)(see figure 2 of that paper). The difference is likely because of the exclusion of production costs of advertising, direct-response advertising, and mobile or social media ads in the Kantar database used in this study. For example, Best Buy reports $840 million Compustat advertising in 2019 but only $229 million in Kantar advertising, as their reported Compustat advertising includes media advertising expense, as well as “agency fees” and “production costs.”13 To observe the incidence of brand asset recognition and the amount of brand value, I use the M&A setting in which acquirer firms recognize brand assets as part of the purchase price allocation. Specifically, I obtain all reported and completed M&As in the United States from 2011 to 2021; I require both acquirer and target firms to be publicly traded from the Bloomberg database.14I then merge these M&As with the above Kantar advertising data, resulting in 371 deals with observable target advertising in pre-M&A periods. For each latter acquisition, I manually collect the purchase price allocation information from the acquirer’s SEC filings (i.e., 10K, 10Q, or 8K) and merge this data with the necessary Compustat and CRSP data. This leads to a final sample of 287 deal observations.15 The primary independent variables are target firms’ average advertising expenditures prior to acquisition.16I use the target firm’s last reported revenue to account for heterogeneity (firm size). Using the Kantar data, I aggregate the advertising expenditures into the following three components, representing independent variables of interest:Traditional(OnlineDisplay) [PaidSearch], defined as the target firm’s average traditional (online display) [paid search] advertising in all years with available data preceding acquisition, scaled by last reported revenue by the target firm, and then multiplied by 1,000 for ease of interpretation. 3.2. The Association Between Advertising Expenditure Type and Brand Asset Recognition I testHypothesis 1(whether target firm traditional and online display advertising has a higher association with the recognition of brand assets versus paid search) using the following logistic model: The dependent variable isBrandRecognition; it is measured as an indicator variable equal to one if the acquirer firm recognizes a separate brand intangible asset on the final purchase price allocation from acquisitioniin yeart+n, and zero otherwise.17 The main variables of interest are the three measures of advertising expenditures:Traditional,OnlineDisplay, andPaidSearch(all bolded). UnderHypothesis 1, if traditional and online display advertising have a larger association with brand development (and thus recognition of a brand upon acquisition), then I predict positive coefficients onTraditional(β1) andOnlineDisplay(β2) that are more positive relative to that onPaidSearch(β3). I control for both target firm and acquirer firm characteristics.18Specifically, I control for the value of brands acquired by target in the past five years before acquisition (TargetBrand), the number of years the target firm has data in Compustat (TargetFirmAge), target industry sales growth (TargetIndGrowth), target three-year sales growth (TargetSalesGrowth), target market share (TargetMktShare), target industry concentration level (TargetIndCompetition), target industry sales volatility (TargetIndDemandRisk), target investment selling, general, and administrative expenses (SG&A) (TargetInvestSGA), and target R&D expense (TargetTech) following prior literature (Bahadir et al. 2008,Enache and Srivastava 2018). The predicted coefficients for the latter variables are all positive, as target firms acquiring more brands, existing for a longer period of time, and having higher industry sales growth, higher individual firm growth, larger market share, higher concentration, less industry sales volatility, and higher investment in SG&A and R&D expense are all expected to have higher cash flow expectations from the acquired brands. FollowingMuller (1999), I also control for a firm’s incentives to over- or undervalue intangible assets by including acquirer’s leverage and financing considerations.AcqLeverageis the acquirer’s long-term debt deflated by total assets; the predicted sign is positive, as firms with higher leverage have incentives to record intangible assets to loosen debt covenant restrictions and thereby increase the firms’ access to new debt (Wyatt 2005).AcqFinancingis defined as the acquirer’s short-term debt scaled by total assets; the predicted sign is positive because firms in need of short-term capital are more likely to disclose discretionary information to reduce the information asymmetry between managers and potential capital providers.AcqSizeis acquirer’s logged total revenue; this proxies for size effects associated with the recognition of brand assets. Finally, I include industry (one-digit SIC) and year fixed effects (FEs) to control for differences in brand across industries and any time-series trend in intangible asset recognitions. 3.3. The Association Between Advertising Expenditure Type and Brand Asset Valuation Next, I assess one key characteristic of recognized brand assets: the valuation amount of the underlying asset. To testHypothesis 2(whether the target firm’s traditional and online display exhibits a stronger association with brand asset valuation versus paid search advertising), I use the following model: The dependent variable isBrandValue, the value of the recognized brand intangible assets deflated by target firm’s last reported revenue, and zero if firm does not recognize a brand asset. I estimate this model using ordinary least squares (OLS). As previously, I control for target’s acquired brand value (TargetBrand), the age of target firm (TargetFirmAge), target industry sales growth (TargetIndGrowth), target three-year sales growth (TargetSalesGrowth), target market share (TargetMktShare), target industry concentration level (TargetIndCompetition), target industry sales volatility (TargetIndDemandRisk), target investment SG&A (TargetInvestSGA), target R&D expense (TargetTech), as well as acquirer short-term and long-term financing need (AcqLeverageandAcqFinancing) and acquirer total assets (AcqSize). All variables are as defined earlier, with the same predicted signs. I again include SIC industry and year fixed effects to control for differences in brand across industries and time. The main variables of interest remainTraditional,OnlineDisplay, andPaidSearch. Consistent withHypothesis 2, if traditional and online display advertising exhibit a larger association with the amount of brand asset that is recognized upon acquisition, then I predict positive coefficients onTraditional(β1) andOnlineDisplay(β2) that are more positive relative to that onPaidSearch(β3).
article.4. Empirical Results: 4.1. Sample Descriptive Statistics Panel A ofTable 1presents the sample selection. The sample includes all completed M&As during 2011–2021 for which both targets and acquirers are U.S. public firms. This yields 1,527 acquisitions. Then, I delete observations of target firms lacking pre-M&A Kantar data. Finally, I manually collect purchase price allocation and valuation process information from the acquirers’ SEC filings; requiring nonmissing data leads to a final sample of 287 M&As. Of these, target brand intangible assets are recognized in 139 (48%) of the M&As. Panel B shows the industry distribution, with approximately 32% (30%) [20%] of either target or acquirer firms operating in the manufacturing (financial) [service] industry. Panel C reports the sample distribution by brand characteristics. Among the 139 deals for which the brand asset is recognized, 19% (24%) [17%] {36%} report an expected useful life of 0–5 years, (6–10 years) [11–38 years] {an indefinite useful life}. Regarding the labels firms use for recognized brands, 2% of firms label “Brand Intangible Assets,” 2% use “Marketing-related Intangibles”, and 1% label “Mastheads,” with the remaining 95% using “Trade names” and “Trademarks.” Lastly, among the 139 deals, 49% do not disclose the method used to value brand intangibles, 1% use the income approach—multiperiod excess earnings method, 38% use the income approach—relief from royalty method, and 12% use the income approach without disclosing the specific method used to value brands. Table 1.Sample Selection and Composition Table 1.Sample Selection and Composition Notes. This table presents the sample selection and composition. Panel A presents the sample selection. Panel B presents the industry distribution for the target and acquirer firms. Panel C presents the distribution by brand characteristics. MPEEM, multiperiod excess earnings method; RFRM, relief from royalty method. Table 2presents the descriptive statistics. Panel A reports the descriptive statistics for the whole sample. Target firms spend 0.22% (0.08%) [0.11%] of total sales on traditional (online display) [paid search advertising]; the average dollar amount spent on traditional (online display) [paid search advertising] is $9.98 million ($0.89 million) [0.44 million]. Panel B reports the descriptive statistics for the brand recognition sample. The advertising variables are, in general, larger than the whole sample, consistent with advertising as a major contribution of brand. Summary statistics for the other control variables are comparable to prior research (e.g.,Bahadir et al. 2008). Table 2.Descriptive Statistics Table 2.Descriptive Statistics Notes. This table presents descriptive statistics. The values of all continuous variables are winsorized at 1% and 99%. All variables are defined inAppendix A. Table 3presents correlations.TraditionalandOnlineDisplayare positively correlated with bothBrandRecognitionandBrandValue, consistent withHypotheses 1and2. The correlations between advertising variables vary from 0.3 to 0.6. Untabulated tests using variance inflation factors suggest no multicollinearity issues either between the advertising variables or the other variables in the analyses. Table 3.Correlations Table 3.Correlations Notes. This table provides the Pearson correlations between major variables used in the regression analysis. Bolded numbers indicate significance at the 10% level. 4.2. The Association Between Advertising Expenditure Type and Brand Recognition Table 4presents the logistic regression results ofEquation (1)(i.e.,Hypothesis 1). Column (1) reports the results without controls and fixed effects. Column (2) presents the results with only control variables. Columns (3) and (4) report the results with both controls and fixed effects. Results from all columns reveal the predicted significantly positive coefficients forTraditionalandOnlineDisplay. Focusing on column (3), an increase of 0.1% of advertising onTraditional(OnlineDisplay) as a percentage of sales leads to an increased probability of 17% (41%) on brand recognition. The coefficient onPaidSearchis negative and insignificant (−0.05;p= 0.24). The table bottom presents tests of coefficients; the differences between bothTraditionalandPaidSearch(p= 0.010) andOnlineDisplayandPaidSearch(p= 0.008) are both significantly positive as predicted. Column (4) presents similar results, now combining traditional and online display. As earlier, the coefficient ofTraditional+OnlineDisplayis significantly positive (0.22;p= 0.00), that onPaidSearchis insignificant (−0.06;p= 0.18), and the difference is significantly positive (p= 0.005). Table 4.The Association of Advertising Expenditure Type and Brand Asset Recognition Table 4.The Association of Advertising Expenditure Type and Brand Asset Recognition Notes. This table presents the logistic regression results ofEquation (1), investigating the association of advertising expenditure types and the recognition of brand assets for firms that are subsequently acquired. The dependent variable isBrandRecognition, an indicator variable equal to one if the acquirer firm recognizes a separate brand intangible asset, and zero otherwise. Industry and year fixed effects are included in columns (3) and (4). Standard errors are clustered at the target firm level.p-values are presented in parentheses. Variables and tests of interest are in bold. ***, **, and * represent significance at the 0.01, 0.05, and 0.10 levels, based on the indicated one-tailed or two-tailed tests of significance. Turning to the control variables, the coefficients onTargetMktShareandTargetIndCompetitionexhibit significantly positive associations with the likelihood of brand asset recognition, consistent with higher brand asset recognition for target firms with higher market share and in more competitive industries. The coefficients onTargetInvestSGAare negatively associated with the likelihood of brand asset recognition. The coefficients onTargetBrandare positive but insignificant. This is likely because of other considerations by the acquirer managers when deciding brand recognition. Brand recognition may depend more on target advertising, target industry competition, and other factors than on the target’s previous brand acquisitions. The remaining control variables are generally insignificant. Collectively, the results confirm that the likelihood of recognizing a brand intangible asset upon acquisition is higher for target firms having traditional and online display advertising versus paid search advertising; this provides evidence consistent withHypothesis 1. 4.3. The Association Between Advertising Expenditure Type and Brand Asset Valuation Table 5presents theEquation (2)results examining the association between advertising expenditures and brand asset valuation (i.e.,Hypothesis 2). Panel A presents the results for the whole sample. Columns (1) and (2) report results without fixed effects. Focusing on column (3), where control variables and fixed effects are included, the coefficient onTraditionalis significantly positive as expected (0.003,p= 0.05), as is that forOnlineDisplay(0.007,p= 0.02). In column (4), the coefficient onTraditional+OnlineDisplayis also significant (0.004,p= 0.00). Regarding the economic effect, an increase of 1% of advertising onTraditional(OnlineDisplay) as a percentage of sales leads to an increased valuation of 0.3 (0.7) on brand as a percentage of target sales. The coefficients onPaidSearchare insignificant (−0.001 and −0.001,p= 0.67 and 0.86, respectively). The differences tabulated at the bottom of the table reveal the coefficients onTraditional(p= 0.097),OnlineDisplay(p= 0.032), andTraditional+OnlineDisplay(p= 0.052) to be larger than that onPaidSearch, as expected. Of the control variables, the target firm’s acquired brand value (TargetBrand) and acquirer size (AcqSize) are consistently significantly positive. The coefficients on the target’s industry sales volatility (TargetIndDemandRisk) are negatively associated with brand value. The remaining control variables are either insignificant or not consistently significant. Panel B presents the results for brand recognition sample, which has 139 observations. Similar to the results in panel A, the coefficients in column (3) onTraditional(0.003,p= 0.09) andOnlineDisplay(0.005,p= 0.09) are significantly positive and are more positive than the coefficients onPaidSearch. In column (4), results usingTraditional+OnlineDisplayare consistent with the previous results. However, the tests of coefficients are directionally consistent in column (3) (p= 0.130 and 0.105) and column (4) (p= 0.123) though insignificant, possibly because of the lower test power with the smaller sample. Table 5.The Association of Advertising Expenditure Type and Brand Valuation Table 5.The Association of Advertising Expenditure Type and Brand Valuation Notes. This table presents the OLS regression results ofEquation (2), investigating the association of advertising expenditure types and brand valuation. Panel A presents results using the whole sample, and panel B presents results with brand recognition sample. The dependent variable isBrandValue, the amount of recognized brand intangible assets deflated by the target firm’s last reported revenue. Industry and year fixed effects are included in columns (2) and (4). Standard errors are clustered at the target firm level.p-values are presented in parentheses. Variables and tests of interest are in bold. ***, **, and * represent significance at the 0.01, 0.05, and 0.10 levels, based on the indicated one-tailed or two-tailed tests of significance. Overall, the results ofTable 5supportHypothesis 2: target firms’ spending on traditional and online display advertising has a more positive association with the intangible brand value recognized by the acquirer firms relative to paid search advertising.
article.5. Sensitivity and Additional Analysis: 5.1. Sensitivity Analyses I next conduct six sensitivity analyses. First, the small sample size may raise the concern of the generalizability of the results. Accordingly, I include private target firms to expand the sample size. After requiring nonmissing deal value and necessary control variables, the final sample consists of 754 deals, of which 468 (62%) recognize brand intangible assets. Because there is no data on target characteristics, deal purchase price is used as the deflator of advertising variables and brand value. Results reveal similar positive associations between traditional and online display advertising with both brand recognition and valuation and that the coefficients of traditional and online display are significantly more positive than that for paid search (see the Online Appendix, Table A.1). Second, as the distribution of three advertising variables exhibits a right skew, with many firms having zero value of at least one of the advertising variables, the results of advertising and brand may be driven by low test power in the small sample. Accordingly, I use an alternative bootstrapping method. Results are unchanged from the primary analyses (see the Online Appendix, Table A.2). Third, because the advertising expense disclosed by firms in the 10K includes more components as compared with the Kantar media advertising used in my analyses, I include an additional control of the target firm’s overall advertising effort. This control is defined as an indicator variable equal to one if the target firm discloses its advertising expense in the 10K, and zero otherwise. Results show that the main inferences are unchanged (see the Online Appendix, Table A.3). Fourth, I include several additional control variables for acquirer and target firm characteristics: M&A synergy, proxied using an indicator variable equal to one if the acquirer and the target are in the same four-digit SIC industry, and zero otherwise; the acquirer’s emphasis on brand, measured by the log value of the number of times the acquirer mentions “brand,” “tradename,” or “trademark” in the most recent 10K preceding the M&A announcement; and the acquirer’s tax incentives, defined as the acquirer firm’s tax expense deflated by its operating cash flows. All the inferences remain unchanged (see the Online Appendix, Table A.4). Fifth, I also adopt an alternative variable definition of firm size (now using the log of the number of employees in the acquirer firm), as well as alternative industry fixed effects (now defined by Fama-French 12-industry classification). All the results are unchanged (see the Online Appendix, Table A.5). Sixth, because the acquirer manager’s decisions to recognize a brand and determine brand value may be highly correlated, I jointly testEquations (1)and (2) using seemingly unrelated regressions. Results show that the coefficients ofTraditional,Online Display, and the sum ofTraditionalandOnline Displayare significantly different than zero, consistent withTables 4and5. The joint test results on the coefficient ofPaid Searchare statistically insignificant (see the Online Appendix, Table A.6). 5.2. Endogeneity Next, I address potential endogeneity within my setting. First, prior research suggests potential endogeneity in advertising response models when the advertising variables correlate with the error terms. In particular, allocation of advertising is not random, suggesting a selection bias can occur when managers choose media channels (Danaher and Dagger 2013). Accordingly, I use propensity score matching to mitigate the differences in target characteristics. Specifically, to identify the treatment group that spends relatively more on traditional and online display than paid search advertising, I calculate the “relative ratio” of paid search advertising divided by the sum of traditional and online display advertising for all target firms in the sample. The target firm is categorized as a treated firm if its “relative ratio” is below the median of its SIC one-digit industry peers. That is, treated firms are defined as those spending relatively more on traditional and online display than paid search as compared with the industry peer targets in the sample. After identifying the treated firms, I estimate the following logistic propensity score model, including all target characteristic variables: I identify matched pairs with the smallest propensity score differences. The final sample has 119 observations, including 87 treated target firms and 32 matched control firms.Table 6presents the results. The coefficients ofTreat, using the matched sample of treatment and control firms, remain significantly positive, consistent with the main results inTables 4and5. Table 6.Endogeneity Table 6.Endogeneity Notes. This table presents the results of the main tests using propensity score matching. The dependent variable in columns (1) and (2) isBrandRecognition, an indicator variable equal to one if the acquirer firm recognizes a separate brand intangible asset, and zero otherwise. The dependent variable in columns (3) and (4) isBrandValue, the amount of brand intangible asset deflated by the target’s last reported revenue.Treatis an indicator variable equal to one if the target firm has a ratio of traditional plus online display to paid search higher than the median among sample target industry peers, and equal to zero otherwise. Industry and year fixed effects are included in columns (2) and (4). Standard errors are clustered at the target firm level.p-values are presented in parentheses. Variable of interest is in bold. ***, **, and * represent significance at the 0.01, 0.05, and 0.10 levels, based on the indicated one-tailed or two-tailed tests of significance. Second, because using propensity score matching results in a reduced sample size, I alternatively use entropy balancing matching to check the robustness of the results. In the first stage, the same “relative ratio” as in propensity score matching is calculated to identify the treatment firms. I then match treatment firms and other firms in the sample based on target characteristics. In the second stage, the obtained matching weights are used in the regression to assess the relationship between target firms, which spend relatively more on traditional and online display advertising, and brand characteristics. Results show significant and positive coefficients ofTreat(see the Online Appendix, Table A.7), consistent with the main results inTables 4and5, as well as the results using propensity score matching inTable 6. In summary, the results using propensity score matching and entropy balancing matching suggest that controlling for the differences in target characteristics, those target firms spending relatively more on traditional and online display than their industry peers are both more likely to have brand recognition and exhibit higher brand valuation.19 5.3. Additional Analyses: Investor Reactions to Brand Assets Finally, I conduct additional analyses to assess whether the recognition of brand intangible assets by managers of acquiring firms reflects perceived value creation by equity investors. Specifically, I examine the market reaction to acquirer firms’ recognized brand values around the date of the public filings in which acquirers recognize brand assets.Kallapur and Kwan (2004)find a positive average stock market reaction to recognized brand amount (using a small sample of 24 UK M&As). I reexamine this issue using my U.S. sample (n= 279), collecting the brand asset amount disclosed in the public filing after the M&A announcement by manually reading all public filings (10Q and 10K) for the acquirer firm. Cumulative abnormal returns (CAR) are calculated using the three-day (−1, +1) value-weighted market-adjusted stock return of the acquirer firm, centered around the filing date of the disclosure.Table 7presents the results. Columns (1), (2), and (3) show the results without interactions. The coefficient onBrandPP(recognized brand assets by the acquirer firm, deflated by the purchase price) is significantly positive across all three columns, suggesting a positive investor reaction to recognized brand amounts. In columns (4), (5), and (6), I interactBrandPPwithTraditional+OnlineDisplayto examine the differential impact of advertising types on the association between brand and market reactions. The coefficients on this interaction term are significantly positive, whereas those onBrandPPare no longer significant. These results indicate that observed positive investor reactions to brand value are mainly driven by acquisitions of target firms, which invest more in traditional and online display advertising. Table 7.Investor Reaction Table 7.Investor Reaction Notes. This table presents additional analysis results of investor reactions to brand asset. The dependent variable is the value-weighted three-day CAR of acquirer firm around the date of the public filing in which the acquirer firm recognizes the brand asset.BrandPPis the brand intangible asset value as reported by the acquirer firm, deflated by purchase price of the deal, which is zero if no brand is recognized. Standard errors are clustered at the target firm level.p-values are presented in parentheses. Variables of interest are in bold. ***, **, and * represent significance at the 0.01, 0.05, and 0.10 levels, based on the indicated one-tailed or two-tailed tests of significance.
article.6. Conclusion: This paper examines how alternative advertising channels (paid search, online display, and traditional) are associated with the financial reporting treatment of brand asset recognition in the context of M&As. The analyses are motivated, in part, by the dramatic shift in advertising expenditures in recent years away from traditional and toward digital channels. To derive the advertising channels, I use a unique data set of annual advertising spending in different media outlets, assessed over the period 2010–2019. Of note, this data set allows decomposition of advertising expenditures into their primary components; prior research either suffers from missing advertising data or uses only aggregated advertising expenses. Consistent with expectations, I find that target firms’ spending on traditional and online display advertising exhibits a larger association with the likelihood of brand asset recognition and the amount of recognized brand value, relative to paid search advertising expenditures. Overall, these results confirm expected heterogeneous effects of different advertising channels on the recognition and valuation of the underlying brand asset. Whereas the paper is written from the perspective of the acquirer managers (reflecting the reporting decision to recognize a brand asset), these findings also offer insights for investors—who are likely to benefit from increased disclosure on advertising and its breakdown into digital and traditional advertising categories, as well as regulators—who may reconsider the financial reporting framework for advertising. Although this paper provides evidence of the association between advertising and brand, firms can also build and enhance brands by investing in R&D and other channels in the modern economy (Govindarajan et al. 2019). Future research can more comprehensively explore the drivers of brand recognition and their effects on valuation. In addition, because of the small sample size and the sample targets containing only firms that successfully are acquired, the results in this study may not generalize to all firms. Future research can explore other settings to further study the effects of advertising and brand on reporting and market outcomes.
